# A02P Clocks & Timers

## ESE 5160: IoT Edge Computing Deadline: Feb 11, 202611:59 PM EST

An **individual** submission on Gradescope.

**Late-day requests:** [https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8](https://forms.gle/7hZVrXLZ51XiCExm8) 

Course Webpage: [https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/](https://ese5160.seas.upenn.edu/)	

Remember to read the [ESE5160 S26 Assignment README](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1DQJxfhTMBN1aE2uasZiKpqR0JOT77pXvb6tUPF7iKF8/edit?tab=t.0#heading=h.c5nyj2pvdk6p) before starting\!

**All the required questions are highlighted in green.**

**Important notes are highlighted in yellow.**

**Very important notes are highlighted in red.** 

# Learning Outcomes

* Get familiar with Silicon Labs Simplicity Studio to configure hardware and generate firmware using the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)  
* Using timer/counter to generate PWM signals and interrupts.  
* Refresh your understanding of oscilloscopes and simple circuit breadboarding.

# Errata

This section includes commonly asked questions about the assignment.  
(Currently nothing)

# Introduction

Timers and counters are a big deal in embedded firmware. You'll see them used in a bunch of different ways \- and you might need them in your final project. This module guides you through using the timer/counter to generate Pulse Width Modulation signals and interrupts to control other components. Let's get started. 🔥🔥

Note:

1. For the remaining assignments, we recommend using Simplicity Studio 6 as your project compiler and flash tool. If you haven’t installed it yet, please do so using this [download link](https://www.silabs.com/software-and-tools/simplicity-studio?tab=getting-started). When installing, please select the full install option. 

Issues installing on your computer? You can use the Penn computer labs:  
*The students can install it themselves \-the installer installs the components in their Windows Account folder.  I signed into an ESE Lab PC this morning and ran the installer (selecting the Full Installation) in my Windows Account (it includes 33 packages) \-when it finishes the suite acquires between 15GBs and 16GBs of space on the system.  That's the problem that I brought up in our discussion.  The installer ran for 5 minutes.*

*When the students launch the Simplicity Studio Console for the first time they may see this dialog box:*  
*![][image1]*  
*Choosing Cancel should be fine.*

*The Group Policy is configured to unused user profiles from those computers once a week.  If the students continue to use the same computer for accessing Simplicity Studio weekly they shouldn't need to reinstall the suite.*

2. If you haven’t installed the Simplicity Studio extension in VSCode, please go to the extensions on your VSCode sidebar and search for Simplicity Studio.   
3. If you don’t have a serial monitor yet, or you find your previous serial monitor too complex to use, you could open the VSCode extension and search for Serial Monitor. After installing, a serial monitor should appear on your panel. If you don’t see the panel, press CTRL \+ \` (on the top left corner of your keyboard). 

# Equipment

These items are available in your team’s locker in first floor of Amy Gutmann Hall (across from the bathrooms)

* Oscilloscope or Saleae Logic Analyzer (see [Saleae Logic Analyzer Guide](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1zHQagA5ZDYBEUysbABl_qmNFuNiJzN3I1dh_9V4C0tM/edit?tab=t.0))  
* USB Type C cable  
* The SiWx917Y development board

| Either BRD2708A Rev 04 ![][image2] | Or [BRD2605A](https://www.silabs.com/development-tools/wireless/wi-fi/siwx917-dk2605a-wifi-6-bluetooth-le-soc-dev-kit?tab=overview)![][image3]  |
| :---- | :---- |

Note before you start part 1 and 2: This board has significantly different timer settings than other microcontrollers you may have used. There are two types of timers: Configurable Timer (CT) and Ultra Low Power Timer (ULPT). Let’s discover how we should use these two timers under different scenarios. 

# 

# 1\. Configurable Timer

| Before you move on, review [the datasheet](https://www.silabs.com/documents/public/data-sheets/siwg917y-datasheet.pdf). See if you can find the answers to the following questions (though submission is not required). How many counters does this Configurable Timer have?  How many bits does each counter have?  How many input signal(s) can it take?  How many output signal(s) can it generate?  Does it support DMA flow control?  Which function(s) should be bound to the input pin of this Configurable Timer?  Which function(s) should be bound to the output pin of this Configurable Timer?  |
| :---- |

You are NOT supposed to change any pin during this assignment. Let’s start with the Configurable Timer. Open Simplicity Studio 6, click on the **Example Projects & Demos** tab, then search for a **timer**. Select the first one (**SL Si91x \- Config Timer Basic and OCU without DMA**). Continue through the pop-ups until the project is created. It is recommended to **Copy contents** when making the project.

![][image4]  
![][image5]  
Open the project in VSCode. You should be able to see this in the Silicon Lab extension. Click the icon that the arrow is pointing to. This will build and flash this example code onto your board. If prompted, select the choice that ends with s37.    
![][image6]  
To validate that the example code is properly running, open your serial monitor and check the output from the board.  
(1.1) Submit a screenshot of your serial monitor.  
You should see something like this:   
![][image7]

Open the file config\_timer\_ocu\_non\_dma\_example.c.  
Try different values for the variable **TIME\_PERIOD\_VALUE**.

(1.2) What do you notice if the number is too high? Why does it happen?  
**Hint:** You should search for this variable and see what it is doing in this file. Think about the number of bits the counter has, and the relationship between this parameter and match\_value. You could even print the match\_value out to see what value it is set to when you adjust TIME\_PERIOD\_VALUE to different values by using DEBUGOUT function.

(1.3) Submit a screenshot of your serial monitor when you set TIME\_PERIOD\_VALUE to 2000\. 

Now, set the example code to run the PWM mode use case. Remember to change the TIME\_PERIOD\_VALUE back to the original value. You should be able to set it in the file config\_timer\_ocu\_non\_dma\_example.h. Double-click the sl\_si91x\_ct\_ocu\_non\_dma.pintool. Check the current pinmap. Do NOT modify the output pin.   
(1.4) Which pin(s) are set to input? Which pin(s) are set to output?

Flash the code to the board. Plug the oscilloscope to the two output pins. [Need a refresher on oscilloscopes?](https://learn.sparkfun.com/tutorials/how-to-use-an-oscilloscope/all)  
(1.5) What is the output frequency and duty cycle of **out0**? Write down what you observe.  
(1.6) What is the output frequency and duty cycle of **out1**? Write down what you observe.  
(1.7) Paste a photo of your oscilloscope capture.

Now, let's try to adjust the frequency and duty cycle of the pwm output. Read through the code and documentation, and adjust the frequency of counter 1 to be 2000Hz, and the duty cycle to be 75%.

(1.8) Demonstrate this functionality to a member of the teaching team during office hours.  
**Hint:** You should see how the match\_value is calculated and used. 

# 2\. Ultra Low Power Timer

| Before you move on, read [the datasheet](https://www.silabs.com/documents/public/data-sheets/siwg917y-datasheet.pdf) online. See if you can find the answers to the following questions (though submission is not required). How many timers does this module have? How many bits does each counter have? What does 1us and 256us mode mean? Hint: Search for General Purpose Timers instead of ULP timers in the datasheet.  |
| :---- |

Let’s come back to this page, and select the last one: **SL Si91x \- ULP Timer**. 

![][image8]

Open the project in VSCode. Flash the code to your chip.  
(2.1) What do you see on the chip? Describe things you notice.

| Think about the difference between ULPT and CT. When should we use the ULPT, and when should we use CT? You will be asked about this during the TA checkoff.  |
| :---- |

# 3\. PWM Generation

In addition to generating PWM using the **CT** directly, you can also generate PWM by installing and using the **PWM** software component provided in Simplicity Studio.  
![][image9]  
The **Motor Control Pulse Width Modulation (MCPWM)** controller for SiW917 is used to generate a periodic pulse waveform, essential for motor control and power control applications. The MCPWM controller functions as a timer to count up to a period count value. Both the time period and the duty cycle of the pulses are programmable.   
![][image10]  
[siw917x-family-rm REV1\_2.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/13-t3d04PsfaUWDGpEd7wK9_sKgnwVVQj/view?usp=drive_link) Page 658

Now, let's get started with the PWM example project.  Open [Simplicity Studio 6](https://docs.silabs.com/ssv6ug/latest/install-ssv6/install-simplicity-studio), click on the **Example Projects & Demos** tab, then search for **pwm**. Carefully read the project documentation and follow the pop-up instructions until the project is created.  
![][image11]  
After flashing the code to your board, use an oscilloscope to measure the PWM output waveform.  
(3.1) Which PWM channel is used in this example? Which pin should you probe to measure the PWM output? **Hint:** Check the Pin Tool configuration & the dev board datasheet[ug590 brd2708a user guide SiWG917Y111MGNBA REV1\_1.pdf](https://drive.google.com/file/d/1tIy1obl0VbL7v7tGwK_fPEyC5HQBHVFf/view?usp=drive_link)

You should observe a waveform similar to the one shown below, with a default frequency of **25 kHz** and a duty cycle of **50%**.  
![][image12]  
After successful program execution, the serial console output should appear as shown below.  
![][image13]

Silicon Labs provides multiple PWM APIs that can be used to configure and update PWM parameters directly.  Please refer to the  [Pwm | Peripherals | WiSeConnect 3 | v3.5.2 | Silicon Labs](https://docs.silabs.com/wiseconnect/3.5.2/wiseconnect-api-reference-guide-si91x-peripherals/pwm).   
Now, change the PWM duty cycle to **80%** by calling the **sl\_si91x\_pwm\_set\_duty\_cycle()** function.

Do **not** modify the SL\_PWM\_CHANNEL\_0\_DUTY\_CYCLE value directly in the **configuration** header.  
The goal of this exercise is to practice updating the PWM duty cycle **at runtime** by calling the PWM APIs in your own code, rather than changing the static configuration.

(3.2) What value should be passed as the duty\_cycle argument?   
**Hint:** What is the current PWM time period?

(3.3) Use an oscilloscope to capture the waveform.  
Remember to add **Frequency** and **Duty Cycle** measurements in your oscilloscope screenshot.

Then, change the PWM frequency from **25 kHz** to **200 Hz**, keeping the duty cycle constant.  
**Think:** Is it necessary to adjust the PWM prescaler to achieve this frequency? Why?  
(3.4) Use an oscilloscope to capture the waveform again.

(3.5) Demonstrate this functionality to a member of the teaching team during office hours. Note that if you are found using the configuration header to modify the pwm output, you will be asked to redo this part. 

# Rubric

See the Gradescope assignment for the rubric details. For all questions, 0 points may be awarded if the submission is non-existent, very poorly done, or doesn’t compile (for firmware assignments).
